sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
She said-- she said she understood that if they take me to bury the cattle, they're gonna bury me together with the cattle.
She said, I'll take the shovel.
And I'll go for him.
Finally, [INAUDIBLE] from the beginning, they have a little bit dignity-- they don't care.
And they don't took me.
Later a while, maybe by half an hour later, they come to me again and said, just we need-- one cow's crawled in-- in that crawling space or something like that.
Once he gets shot, you got to be pulled out and buried you-- and buried you.
OK.
We went.
A few guys, we pull it out.
And we pull it in-- in a big hole from a bomb.
We try to bury the-- that-- cover it up with some sand.
And that lady's brother, he was there, too.
He find a gun.
And he come to me.
And he show it to me.
Look what I find.
I said, hide it.
Hide it so no-- no one will see.
This way, somebody going to see it, you're-- you're gonna be shot.
He hide them.
And then we get rid of them.
When I left, I left.
And then I went to [INAUDIBLE].
When I come to [INAUDIBLE], I was hiding in the garden between the potatoes, because I don't want to go to work for them.
Finally, I was [INAUDIBLE] for three weeks.
I can't, alone, can't stay there.
And then I come out.
And one time, I get dressed.
I told-- I told them I'm going to see my friends.
When I come close to-- not far from my friend's house, a German saw me.
And I said [INAUDIBLE].
I stop.
And he took me away in the synagogue-- to clean the synagogue.
Down there was so dirty.
It was terrible.
I wear brown pants with a white shirt.
When I come out from down there from the synagogue, my pants was black wool-- fleece.
I-- I couldn't go straight home.
I went straight to the river.
And from the river, I took a bath.
And I-- I clean up myself because it was terrible.
Some kind of gypsies down there, the Germans lock it up.
And it was so dirty.
Terrible.
Finally, they just take us to-- to work every day, every day in another place.
And we used to work, some Jewish groups and Polish groups-- not together.
On the highways.
Mostly on the highways.
Sometime and another places are mostly on the highways because the highways was broken from tanks and tractors.
And we used to just fill up with gravel.
When I was working on the highway, I was maybe about 80 people-- Jewish people-- working.
And was a guard was watching us.
And I see the guard is coming straight to me.
He came to me.
And he said, I see you understand what you're doing.
You are a good worker.
You know-- you know what you're doing.
He said, come here.
Go on the end.
You wanted to sit down, you can sit down.
No.
You wanted to stay, you stay.
You want to work, you can work.
You do not want to work, you can work either.
You can do anything.
Just don't sit down.
He said, from my side, you can sit down, either, or the other guys on the motorcycle is gonna beat you up for that.
OK.
Then I was doing that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that work.
Finally, by-- before Rosh Hashanah, finally they start to take out-- take-- take over the-- Jewish people drived to the synagogue.
Was a marketplace.
[INAUDIBLE] stop.
Some people, they put them in the synagogue.
And some people they put in that marketplace.
Yeah.
When I saw this, I say, I'm not going to wait for that.
They're going to lock me up in the market.
I just left.
It wasn't-- they-- I didn't [? get ?] shooting, here and there.
Oh, I see that the shooting's not so danger.
And I came to Nacza.
I came to Nacza.
And then my uncles, too, came to Nacza on-- And we live in Nacza in our place, in our house.
Yeah.
And the uncles, too.
We lived till the end, till we got to go to Radun in ghetto.
We had, from before, some food prepared.
They have everything going on before.
Was pretty hard.
We survive.
Anyway, then we been in the-- in-- we went to the ghetto.
And in the ghetto, we used to go to work, too, sometime on the highway, sometime on the road, sometime in the woods-- but mostly in the woods.
Radun.
Still '41.
And we used to go-- they used to have a [INAUDIBLE] to put in, like the Germans give us three cubic meter a day.
[INAUDIBLE] Two men, three-- three cubic meter wood.
And so it's-- the war was in our area.
Before, some Polish soldiers, they went to the war.
And they don't come back.
Some men-- and everyone got to make that [INAUDIBLE].
We used to make 10 cubic meters a day, me and my brother.
Used to make 10 cubic meters a day.
They used to give three to the-- to the Germans and seven to people.
We used to get food for that.
If not, we couldn't survive.
That's-- we work out.
And I used to sharpen the saw.
I got a good, Swedish saw.
And I used to sharpen myself.
And we used to work hard.
But we lived not bad, because we give to the people, like, seven cubic meter a day.
And we have lots of people that need this.
And they give us food, meat, and bread-- everything, you know?
So were you living in the Radun ghetto at this time?
No.
We was-- we were staying for all week in-- in a village to work in-- in the woods.
Yes.
Ejszyszki.
[INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
I remember their names.
Finally, one time they stopped to check the passports in Radun ghetto.
The-- the Germans, the Germans and the police.
And they pick it up who wasn't from Radun-- the-- [INAUDIBLE] from Radun the passport.
They take them away.
And we were-- I told you before, I was lucky.
Our house-- they don't check.
They don't walk in.
They don't check.
Because we are from [INAUDIBLE], and they don't check our passports.
Maybe we're taken away, too.
Naturally, they picked up over [? 40 ?] people.
And they-- was February.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think was in February, the coldest weather what I remember.
And they shot 43 people-- men, women, and children.
And they give us two hours to bury the people.
I was-- they took me to bury the people.
Who can break the ground, three foot frozen ground?
Finally, we find a place where a bomb was.
And we bring the people down there.
And still, some people was living.
You've got to take him out, somehow.
For some-- some, they took it out a couple people, persons, I think, after they died.
On-- because I don't know how they are counting, after.
We were just cover up with snow.
And they scare us.
If you don't bury them for two hours, they're gonna shoot more.
Did you know any of the people who were shot?
Oh, yeah.
Some I remember.
Not-- not too many.
I used to know lots of them, the people, like I said.
I don't know their names, exactly.
No.
I forgot all the names.
I used to know them, lots of them.
Some of them I don't.
Lots of them are-- finally, we buried the people.
And when we came-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's a warmer, [INAUDIBLE] it start to stink.
And then we've got to take them out and bury them in another place.
Finally, before Pesach, yeah.
Before Pesach, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we used to bake matzos sometime, in the house.
We [INAUDIBLE].
We brought them to-- in the ghetto.
And we think to bake matzos.
We got the equipment.
And we put out all together.
We know how to do it, me and my brother.
Finally, we prepare the flour.
We prepare everything.
We've got the equipment what everything.
We don't have any wood.
And to bake matzos, if the German catch us-- dead.
What we did, me and my younger brother, we went with a saw outside the ghetto.
It was some poles from my friends.
We cut it down, maybe about 10 poles.
It was hard, dry oak.
And we brought in.
We cut and we split them, everything.
And we bake some matzos.
We have cutters cutting the-- the-- I know, we have a lot of guts, I'm telling you.
We-- we had three wood cutters to-- cutting the poles or baking the matzos-- all the [INAUDIBLE] dead.
Everything, no punishment.
Just dead.
And then, finally, we had the matzos.
After the matzos, pretty soon they surrounded the ghetto.
And [? soon, ?] this is the end.
This was in '42 already. '42.
It was in May.
About half a year, something like that.
And my older brother was in-- in the woods.
And just was waiting to hide somewhere or to run away or something, you know?
Oh, he was-- he was working about a week.
When the-- the-- the-- all the Germans brought-- the police brought back all the workers back in the ghetto.
And I said to him, why you come back?
He said, I was worried about you.
I said, we'll manage somehow.
I said, you have a chance.
You-- you-- you should not coming back.
He said, I could go away.
Just, I was worried.
Finally, we find a place.
We hide ourselves.
It was like on top-- on top another building, and then like a little roof.
We crawl in down there, in that place.
And we saw everything.
There were our four brothers-- my younger brother, my sister, they don't go down there because they was not well.
And then some cousins was down there.
We hear everything they took away.
We heard the shooting, everything.
After they [INAUDIBLE] the shooting, [INAUDIBLE], they left some people to work.
And somebody said, who was hiding?
Got to go and register.
Got to go and register.
We come out.
We went down there.
We registered, by the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then it get dark.
Not all of them get registered.
And they said on the next day, they going to register again.
And the next day, everybody who wasn't registered was shot.
And me, and my three brothers and a cousin, they pick us out to pick up the bad people that try to run away.
They give us all some [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE].
And we start to pick up the people.
We picked up about 80 people.
No one [INAUDIBLE].
I walk in.
I saw a woman sitting with a baby, about a year old.
[PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] [CRYING] Yeah.
They was even both shot.
The woman was sitting dead, and the baby, holding the baby, dead.
And by the baby was hidden out a piece, a chunk of meat from an animal.
I don't know, from a cat or skunk or something like that, some kind of wild animal.
Naturally, ?] I put it in the wagon.
And we pick out all the dead people.
And we are talking about-- I think it's all, they're all picked up.
Finally, I see a man is walking with a blanket-- thrown over in a-- in a blanket on a [INAUDIBLE] baby.
They was hiding in that place about 30 people.
It was about a month old, the baby.
And the baby start to cry.
And they start to yell, do something to the baby.
Finally, the father quieted down the baby.
And when he brought the baby, give it to me handle, to me.
And he said, I'll get murdered for my baby.
I'll ?] [? get murdered ?] for my baby.
And behind him was standing a little girl.
And she was looking at me, so sad.
[CRYING] And actually a little-- this little girl-- [INAUDIBLE].
You hear about her?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I took the baby.
And I put the baby in the wagon.
[INAUDIBLE] we used to take to the-- to the pits.
And then they said, another dead person.
I said, we pick up all of them.
He said, your cousin.
That cousin what was picking up together with us the dead people-- one guy was from-- he from [INAUDIBLE].
And one guy was from [INAUDIBLE].
He was a policeman.
And when they was going in school, there was fighting in school.
And he said, he's a Communist.
They- they shot him.
And then we picked up--
Who?
Who shot who?
[INAUDIBLE].
I know the name.
Pat-- Pat Bartoszewicz.
Yeah.
Yitzchok.
Asner.
Finally, when we took all the dead people, the-- the people saw us.
And they know us down there.
And we said, run away.
The police-- [INAUDIBLE] Run away.
Run away.
We was waiting till the night.
And my brothers, they went first.
And I-- I don't know the reason why I left the last one, on the next day.
I-- I don't remember why.
Just I left the last one.
And I just, what I do, I said, I went and I took the addresses from United States for my uncle.
I said, maybe we'll survive some time.
And we'll get in touch sometime.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was ready to leave.
And I saw the Sefer Torah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I said, I cannot leave-- leave this Sefer Torah like this.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wrap it up, the Sefer Torah, and I find like a double ceiling.
And I took the Sefer Torah, I push it in down there.
And I left.
I don't know.
Maybe it's still even laying down there.
No.
I know I would recognize the house.
That was in Radun, the ghetto.
Anyway--
Just at-- at nightfall, around [INAUDIBLE].
That's it.
It wasn't [INAUDIBLE] guarded because the people's already killed.
Finally, and lots of people, they were the leftover-- the leftovers from the ghetto.
They was looking on us.
We are-- we are from [INAUDIBLE].
We are so-- boys, practically.
We know everything throughout we've been in the war.
Said that these things, they was looking after these things, too.
Finally they left.
Lots after us left, too.
Somehow they-- they run away.
Oh, before we left, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a couple girls.
But they know the chief of police.
And the chief of police said to them, find some young man.
But they're [INAUDIBLE] out being in the woods.
And we'll be in contact with you in case some kind of danger.
We'll let you know with the chief of police-- that's the chief of police.
Finally, we agreed.
And we went, about 30 people.
Oh, before that, when I used to go to work, there was a one man, a tall guy [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know.
I come in the woods.
He was working in the-- in the forest rangers, somehow, he got some kind of position.
And he-- and I was looking at him.
And I hate him.
He was like [INAUDIBLE].
You can't talk to him anything.
When we went in the woods, and they said, you going to send us a contact, and I see that man is the contact, I was surprised.
I figured he biggest Nazi in the world, no?
Finally, he used to come to us and tell us all the news and everything.
And we don't know nothing about him.
And I was surprised he is our contact.
What was his name?
It was Antoni Rozak.
Tadeusz Lugowski.
Oh, yeah.
They-- they-- they got killed.
Mary Feldman only a brother, two brothers.
No, a brother and a sister Feldman.
And another couple girls.
Anyway, with that, only come in a couple times.
One time he come in-- that time, we'd have no idea from partisans.
We don't think about partisans that time.
But still, we was thinking to get some weapon to defend ourselves.
And one time, like I see on his face, he wanted to tell us something.
Only still-- so [INAUDIBLE], I don't know.
And he was afraid to go.
And we said to him, me and my brother-- with my older brother.
Younger one was [INAUDIBLE] We said, Antoni, you want to like escort you to-- to your village?
He said, yes.
He was afraid for-- for-- for the wind.
He was afraid.
Finally, they came too close to the village, was some kind of broken trees and everything.
See?
He said Germans done this.
It was getting dark.
I said, where are you going to see Germans?
There's no Germans down there.
He said, there.
He said, on the broken trees.
He was thinking German.
Finally, maybe that little story we read in-- in the house.
And a few days later he was arrested.
And they took him away.
We found out his name was [?
Aron ?] Rosenthal.
If he would know, even want to tell us or [INAUDIBLE] us, he was Jewish.
And somebody squealed on him and they take him away and never saw him back.
Then they send us another guy.
It was, I think, summertime-- late summer.
Yeah.
I don't know nothing about him.
Just you know, after we find out he was [?
Aron ?] Rosenthal.
The-- the chief of police.
He send a replacement.
His name was Czeslaw Wieckowski.
And he was from the beginning.
And finally, we saw something is not right.
So-- but then, the-- one-- that time he said something.
But we don't like it.
And one time he said, the Polish underground caught him.
And he was arrested by them.
And he ran away.
I, like a ex-soldier, and everything is not so easy to run away from that kind of thing.
And the [INAUDIBLE] you start to keep an eye on him.
Got to keep an eye on.
Finally, the-- then the was attack on our group.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was attack on our group.
And killed, I think, about-- about 10 people that time.
And a few ran away.
And they met.
And they ran away in a place.
They met a guy, what he said, they got some more people in the woods where they'd been, already, a winter.
Five people-- Russian military men.
All military, Russian.
And they set up a meeting with-- with us.
And he was living in the [INAUDIBLE] because, down there, they took over a piece of land.
And they include the [INAUDIBLE].
We wasn't like in [INAUDIBLE] Russia.
Finally, we set up a meeting with them.
We have already started to do it a little bit work.
And he saw us.
And he really like us, that-- that-- that leader of the group.
And he was a lieutenant from the army, from the Russian army.
When he was the secretary of the Communist party.
Ivan-- Ivan Ivanovich Stepenko.
And he really like us.
Finally, we went with him.
Then they move to [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] was doing a little bit work, little by little, to-- to spread some kind of news to the-- he was a very talented man.
News.
And finally, we have some cousins in the woods, and-- too, in the-- in the village.
And finally, they-- my cousins, they-- they killed the father and the brother.
They killed both of them.
We don't know.
And then finally, they-- they told us to find the cousins.
We went to look for them.
We don't find them.
Me and my brother, no.
We find two men that was waiting for us, because when the winter supposed-- they are afraid for the winter.
Some people afraid for the winter.
They went back to the ghetto.
And when they start to kill in Grodno ghetto, they run back to the woods again.
And they know-- they've been already in the summertime-- they know in that place they will meet somebody.
Finally, I know the wood people for quite a while.
And they start to beg us to show the place.
They don't need our help, just to show they going to be close to us.
Finally, when we come in, the-- the-- we took him-- from the beginning, I said, we can-- we've got to ask permission.
It's not allowed to do it.
And then they start to beg and everything.
We know them.
They're Jewish.
We brought them in in our place.
And down there was playing a little girl, was [?
Tanya. ?] Found her too, in the woods.
She was belonging to the-- to that leader in the-- from the group.
I have a thing [INAUDIBLE] down there.
And she went down there.
And she said, Abel and Jacob brought two men.
Abel and Jacob brought two men.
Then the leader comes up.
He was our leader.
When I was laying down to rest like in a doghouse, like a doghouse.
And my brother was drying the-- the socks.
We was wondering all night by the fire.
When you start to give him hell with the language, that's a real language.
And my brother don't answer anything.
And I laying [INAUDIBLE].
That's shaken me.
That kind of language for what, I think.
Why we brought the two people.
And they've been Communist, them two-- two people, because they are Jewish.
And I see her swearing on-- on my brother with all kinds rough-- and my mother, mother, mother.
And answer them.
They said to him, you better shut up.
And I told him the same thing.
I-- he get same lesson.
But I have [INAUDIBLE] took out the pistol.
And I said, I'm pretty good at target.
I said, don't think I'll make a mistakes, too.
If you want to try it out, let's try it out.
We'll find out.
Finally, he said, I'm-- it's not going anywhere.
And finally, they went back.
And then, already, they-- I wasn't belonging to the Communist party.
It's such a long story.
OK.
OK.
